CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The ‘‘ Irish Times”’ has opened its editorial mouth
on the Mansfield case, it has taken sides against Mans-
field. Ite article is cunning though not convincing.
Oh, what a Press! The “ Independent,’” that did not
see a sensation in the Partridge dismissal by the Great
?ourface Railway, is trying to do the hero on the cheap
over the Mansfield case, and judging by reports of ex-
pressions of thanks, some green teachers redlly seem

* to accept it that William. Martin. Murphy, tramway-
man, railway-man and haberdasher, is a disinterested
hero gone out to fight for justice for the teachers!
The’ cunning “‘ Trish Times’” doesn’t. see business in
the Mansfield case, and so-it approaches the case quite

dishonestly and drops down on the Starkie side of the
fence. We conclude that it approaches the question
dishonestly because of the different manner in which
it treated the Carsonite bluff. In connection with
Carsonism, the ethics, the constitutionalism, were
minor matters, the great thing that “‘Alf "’ insisted on
was the grim ° —alleged fact we would say—
that the Carsonites were to do this, that, and the other:
when it comes to the Mansfield case the ‘‘ Trish Times"’
argues like a tame official, it looks at the case as if it
were one of'Starkie’s ‘‘ pets.”’

The ** Trish Times ' tries to bolster up Starkie by
imputing party politics to the agitation that the Mans-
field dismissal has aroused. For instance, it says:—
“‘An obvious political motive lies behind a great deal
of the present agitation.” = The political motive is
certainly not obvious to us, and with all due disrespect
to Arnott’s cunning paper, we doubt if it is obvious to
it. The *“ Irish Times” argues for discipline in public
services, and in the abstract we all agree that discip-
line must be maintained if the machinery of public
service is to work. Of cowrse under Home Rule dis-
cipline in the public services, under the control of the
Trish Parliament, must be kept. The cunning ‘‘ Trish
Times’’ will not deceive many, if any, people with or-
dinary brains by attempting to put up the National
Board, Starkie and his *‘ pets,”” as constituting a nor-
mal public service. The whole thing is abnormal and

The Board is responsible not even to the
liament, and in view of the letters by Mr.
Purser and Mr. Beatty, we cannot understand how

y man of spirit remains upon the Board unless Star-

kieism is fully inquired into.

The putting up of the National Board, which seems
to mean in practice Starkie and his *‘ pets,” as a nor-
mal authority over a great public service is something
on a par with all the catch cries about the sacredness
of contract, the rights of property, and what not that
were hurled against the Land League when it wenb
into grips, not with property, but with that special
type of landlordism in Ireland over the defeat of which
even anti-Irish Unionists now talk rejoicing platitudes.
If Clanrickarde may be said to stand for property, we
are willing to agree that Starkie and his ** pets '’ may
be said to represent the authority of a public service.
Starkie and his ‘' pets,”” like landlordism of the Irish,
or anti-Trish, kind must go: they are both abnormal
and a public danger. The * Irish Times,” having
jumped to the Carsonite tune, having in face of law,
order, and common sense put up what it called the
““fact "’ of the grim bluffers as the determining factor
in the Home Rule controversy, cannot now so easily
slide into a bogus ultra-constitutional position with re-
gard to Starkie and his ‘‘ pets.” We agree that dis-
ciplipe must be maintained and authority must be up-
held in normal public services, and that the undermin-
ing of either discipline or authority is a danger to the
commonweal. ‘But the abnormal National Board,
which in practice means Starkie and his ““ pets,” is a
menace and a serious one to that healthy public spirit
that stands, and ought to stand, in favour of discipline
and authority under normal conditions of public ser-
vices. Indeed every patriotic Irishman of conserva-
tive bias—and we Irish”are all rather of the conserva-
tive type—must see in this absurd Board a_ greab
danger to the principle of authority and of discipline.
The Board is an impossible working machine in_this
country. It has produced on one side Starkie and his

‘“ pets’ as the real repository of all authority, and on
the other-it has goaded men like Mansfield into—call
it technical insubordination if you like. The fallacious
quibbles of the ** Irish Times” will not blind intelligent
people to the realities of the situation: and in any case
the ** Irish ‘Times’ >’ performance in catching hold of
what it was pleased to call the ‘ fact "’ in the Carson-
ite treason-humbug puts it out of court.

Can you get a Belfast jury to convict in a case in-
volving anti-Irish Belfast lawlessness and blackguard-
ism? Counsel for the Crown applied to Bccor ¥
Craig of Belfast to have certain Belfast atrocity 2ases
sent on for trial to the Assizes which will be held in
Derry this year. Recorder Craig would not accede to
this application, even in face of the view of the Crown
Solicitor that a fair trial was not to be had in Belfast.
We wonder what does Peter the Packer from
think of Recorder Craig of Belfast of the atrocities!
The first, and, as it proved, the last, batch of prisoners
tried before the wise and impartial Judge Craig in Bel-
fast were very quickly acquitted by a Belfast jury.
Then the Crown threw up the sponge and decided not
to go on with the other cases. This is Belfast—after
the atrocities. Is it any wonder that Belfast rather
stinks in the nostrils of Ireland just now, and that some
of its commercial travellers are having, as it were, ver-
dicts found against them in ‘ Poverty Province,’
‘“ Maynooth Province’” and ‘‘ Land League Province.”

The question of Fire Insurance came up before the
_Dungarvan Board of Guardians recently. Mr. John
Walsh was against it. ‘‘ They did not know who this
Trish company was.”” Well, Mr. Walsh can scarcely
plead invincible ignorance for he can easily -find out if
he wants to know. The resolution to give the insur-
ance to the Irish company was lost by 20 votes to 11.
The Mr. Walsh who was dead against the Irish com-
pany then proposed a high-sounding resolution about
the Mansfield case. Mr. Mansfield, said Mr. Walsh,
was a brilliant Irishman: well, that is more than we
can say of Mr. Walsh. This eloquent and cheap reso-
lution was of course passed unanimously by this meet-
ing of Guardians, the majority of whom refused to sup-
port an Irish Insurance company. The Dungarvan
5 i Rural Couneil had decided to insure with the Hiber-

- nian, but Mr. P. Flynn moved that the arrangement be
rescinded. The motion to rescind was carried by 19
votes to eight. The chairman stated that on the occa-
sion when the insurance was given to the Hibernian
s the members present were unanimous, though Mr.
: O’Sullivan denied this. However that may be, the In-
surance was given to the Hibernian and now it was
taken from it by 19 votes to eight. There’s stability
for you.

At the meeting of the Guardians a bank manager
appeared to be the pivot upon which many members
turned: and it was said that this bank

~was able to whip in men that day whom they had not

seen for months. However that may be in this parti-

cular case, the interference of bank managers in these
‘insurance affairs appears to be a great evil. If a bank

: manager is interested in some piece of business in the
_ gift of a local Council it is easy to understand that
s . members who may be under an obligation to him, or
rather to his bank, are in a nasty position. We wonder
how many of the Guardians who refused to give the
. insurance to the Hibernian were in a position to snap
their fingers at the particular bank manager in the case.
 As we understand these cases, there is no need that

A correspondent has sent us a circular issued by a
Belfast Preserve Factory. Evidently the business of
this factory in ““ the South and West ’’ has not been
improved by the Belfast atrocities. The circular com- -
mences : ‘‘ We are informed that a report has been cir-
culated abroad that we have dismissed our Catholic
hands. We must emphatically deny this, which is a

IRISH - MADE BEDOING
AND FURNITURE.

Wool, Fibre, Hair and Spring Mattresses, Feather
Beds, Bolsters and Pillows, Kitchen, Parlour,
Drawing Room Furniture manufactured from the

BEST AND PUREST MATERIALS.

All Goods Manufactured and Guaran-
teed by us bear the Irish Trade Mark.

- malicious falsehood.”” We are given no evidence as to
the truth' of the information that this report has be-n
circulated. Who has circulated it? Very probably i

- representatives of Belfast industries, who may find that
. the Belfast atrocities and the fact that the ** South and
- West * have been subscribing to a fund for the vietims,

have got the backs of customers up, rush to the con-
- clusion, without any further evidence, that reports are
being circulated about them. Surely the Belfast atroci-
ties, the fact that the *“ South and West ** is called upon
to subscribe, and has subscribed, to a fund for the
victims of Belfast blackguardism and intolerance,
the slanders on the ‘‘ South and West,”' the insults
hurled at Ireland from Belfast, the wish of Belfast to
be excluded from Ireland under Home Rule are suffi-
cient to account, and naturally account, for a shyness
on the part of many in the slandered and insulted
* South and West " to fill the order books of Belfast
travellers. The firm to which we vefer print in their
circular a statement signed by one of their
workingmen in presence of a magistrate, stat-
ing that he, a Catholic, is at present and has been
for over 19 years in the employment of the firm, that
there are other Catholics—the number of them is not
mentioned—in the employment of the fin also, and
that he had never known the firm to discharge anyone
on account of religion. But is not this a tinkering way
of attempting to meet the case? So far as we can make
out the worm—the ‘‘ South and West *'—has given a
little turn, and as a consequence some individual firms
are writing letters or otherwise individually meeting
their end of the case. Surely the statesmanlike thing
for Belfast wholesale and manufacturing houses is to
convene a meeting of their representatives and publicly,
and with their names attached, denounce the Belfast
atrocities, repudiate the slanders on the rest of Ireland,
declare that they are part of Ireland and have no sym-
pathy with those who seek to have Belfast and neigh-
*bourheod cut out of Ireland.

““ Colman ”’ writes:—‘‘ Dear- Sir,—Mr. Bullick’s
letter in your last i must give satisfaction to your
readers in so far as it regards his own firm, and which
I accept as quite true, though I know nothing person-

~ ally about Messrs. Hicks, Bullick and Co. However,
I must take exception to the following statement con-
tained in the letter :—* We have not heard of any case
of the like (intimidation) except in the shipyards.” No
doubt Mr. Bullick is a good man who goes to ‘ meet-
ing,” or ‘church,” or ‘army,” or etc., and reads his
‘Telegraph’ or ‘ Newsletter’ and minds his business.
Hence he is an ‘ Innocent at Home.” Here are a few
facts for him. Eight Catholic reelers were driven by
almost 100 hooliganesses, by cursing the Pope, singing
‘ Dolly’s Brae,” by unspeakable language about priests,
ete., from ‘Owen o’ Cork’ Mill, Beersbridge Road.
He can get all information from Carmichael’s, 49 Short
Strand.  These expelled women went on a deputation
to the authorities at the ‘Owen o’ Cork,’ but they would
not be heard. Young Cullen 10 (?) Foundry Street,
was beaten at the Rope Works and the case was proved
in court. Duff, R.M., said it was a quarrel- among
boys and dismissed it. Cullen, one of the chief bread-
wimmers of the family, could get no work. A girl

® called Kearney was beaten at the Rope Works and -
- could not go back through fear.

Kearney’s live in
Khartoum Street, Anybody will point out the house
to Mr. Bullick. I could give almost a hundred driven
from the Rope Works, and many of them are not back
‘yet. Others are across the seas. Then he will find
Tom Connolly, 1 Khartoum Street, who was told by

THE LEADER.

Myr. Fox, of Davidson’s Sirrocco Works, that no more
Catholics would be employed; he will find young
McKenna at the end of the same street, driven from
Davidson’s also. Police protection had to be got for
the Catholic workers at ‘ Tay Lane Mill,” and at Mur-
ray’s Tobacco Factory. I must say Murray’s did a
great deal to protect their Catholic workers, but they
should have closed down to do so efficiently. Did Mr-
Bullick ever hear of Coombe, Barbur, etc.  If Mr.
Bullick goes into the above he may lose some of his
innocence and I can give more if he wishes.”

The *“Irish Times’’ mentioned Gaelic football in
its Monday’s issue. Probably it was the first time
that it ever referred to a Gaelic match in ifs sporting
news columns. To the great match at Jones’s Road
on Sunday, at which about 20,000 people were present,
it gave—three lines! There’s an “‘ Irish’’ newspaper -
for you.

Rev. Cornelius Treacy, C.SS.R., N. Perth, West
Australia, in the course of a letter, dated October 7th,
just to hand, says:—*‘ Kind friends in Ireland send me
your paper. The LEADER is welcome in Australia. It
is read in the cities, it is a thoughtful companion in the
lonely Bush.' God bless you, and God bless your mighty
work for the land you are preparing to be ‘ A Nation
once again ’ in every aspect.”’

‘“ Howth ”” writes:—“I spent half an hour on
Saturday evening last—9 to 9.30 p.m.—in the picture
theatre, Talbot Street. The performance during that
time represented a perfumed (I suppose he was per-
fumed) Anglicised looking cad, with the inevitable
cane in his hand, trying to coerce a fisherman’s wife
at Howth to be untrue to her husband. Such a ridi-
culous and improbable thing to suggest in connection
with the innocent fishermen of Howth and their wives!
Strangers who know not Ireland, or at least have only
an imperfect conception of the virtue of its inhabi-
tants, might possibly go away with the idea that such
silly’and wicked incidents are characteristic of Howth
fishermen and fishing villages generally in Ireland.
The fisherman’s wife treated with contempt the.dis-
gusting importunities of the would-be destroyer of her
home and peace. When he could not succeed other-
wise he hired a boat and bribed two fishermen (they
might be Arklow men as Arklow men frequent Howth
in the fishing season) to induce the lady’s husband to
sea and throw him overboard, which they did, but he
was rescued on the point of being drowned by two
guards, restored to his home and brought to con-
sciousness. While this diabolical scheme was being
carried out, the would-be wrecker of the innocent
Howth fisherman’s home and family bribed two other
fishernien to administer gas to the absent fisherman’s
_wife and her little son, about four years cld, and carry
them off to a place of seerecy; but they very soon
recovered consciousness when the. man of black and
base designs came on the scene once more to renew
his wicked entreaties, and, when scorned away, locked
the door upon them. The fisherman’s wife disco-
vered a rope and threw it out of an upper window,
put her little son out on the roof, who slid down by
the rope and gave the alarm. The designing black-
guard of the plot again entered thie place and acted
rudely towards the woman, while almost simultane-
ously a fisherman, accompanied by her husband, ran
in and seized the blackguard.  After a sharp en-
counter the dastard came to earth apparently stabbed,
and the fisherman also fell seemingly wounded, and
a most vevolting, incredible and atrocious libel upon

- the poor ha.l‘d‘working fishermen of Howth and their

‘simple wives disappeared from the screen. The fisher-
men of Howth little imagine that anyone would think
of representing them to.the public as capable of such

* treacherous and rascally deeds, ready to betray their

comrade fisherman in & most sacred matter for a bribe,

* even to the length of committing murder to effect such

a gross and outrageous purpose. There.was a large
audience,. who in the main applauded the Lriumphs. of
virtue. Why on earth is such a grossly sensual thing
put up for the amusement of the people, or what pur-
pose could it serve except to do the devil’s work ? Is
it possible that the dirty minds that can conjure up
such loathsome positions of filth and meanness ima-
gine that the people are so saturated with ignorance
and evil inclinations as to derive enjoyment from
them ? One thing is certain, we are dreadfully tame
in the face of such insult.” 2

There was the usual nauseating flood of tolerance
proving at Waterford a few evenings ago when a clerk
in the employ of the notorious Provincial Bank was
presented with an illuminated address and testimonial.
The bank clerk, we are sure, melted the heart of every
‘‘tame’’ present by telling them that they were not
bigots. He said :—‘ There had been much talk about
religious intolerance all over Ireland, and especially in
the South and East. He stood there as a proof
against that. He came there some years ago, one of

- the minority, and he should say he received more kind-

ness and more loyalty from his Catholic neighbours

“and friends than from all the other creeds in that part

of Ireland. He should say that there was no such
thing in this city as religious intolerance. Iiveryone
had a right to his own feelings and opinions, and in
Waterford that right was gladly given.” And a
speaker, who is an *‘ Idolater,”” subsequent ced
that :—*‘ He was glad to emphasise what Mr. Tilson
had said in reference to the question of religion. The
last thing that entered their minds was any question
of religious differences. No such question entered into
business in the South, in that city, and he could go
further and say County Kilkenny, Tipperary and Wex-
ford. He was glad that that expression should come
from Mr. Tilson, and it was a pleasing sign of the
times.”” Of course, a moment’s reflection will show
—but ** tames’” do not reflect under these circum-
stances—that this is all one-sided. To go no further
than Waterford, does not the question of religion affect
people’s chances in Strangman’s brewery or in
Denny’s bacon factory? And what of the notorious
Provincial Bank?  We believe that Denny’s never
employed an ‘‘ Idolater’”’ in their offices; and some

years ago when a clerk in their employment became -

Sergeant Waterman has left Pomeroy for BeJfu.:sﬁ,
and recently a deputation of some of the free and in-
dependent, statesmen of J:_‘omeg'oy ran after the bobby
to Belfast and presented him with an address and purse *
of sovereigns. The signatures to the ud(‘lresﬁ to this
policeman include :a()ol.ALowry, J.P.; Capt. Lowry,
V.L., J.P.; Capt. Goslin, l{.M.; Rev. W. Glenn,
M.A.; Rev. W. Kelly, B.A.; Dr. Mayne, Messrs.
Peter Shields, J.P.; J. S. Hughes, J.P.; J. F. Grimes,
J.P., and John Bllison, C.P.S. Peeler Waterman, in
spite of his name, was, we hear a great favourite with
the Bungs of Pomeroy. Pomeroy Bungs, or some of
them, do a fine trade on Sundays. We hear that a
local Catholic, accompanied by the Master of the local
Orange l.odge, went round seeking for subscriptions.
J. I'. Grimes, J.P., is a newly appointed Cafholic
magistrate : Shields is a J.P. for some years, and the
rest of the names mentioned belong, we beliove, ‘to
Protestants. Grimes, by the way, is a great man for
making speeches in connection with the U.L.L.

T'rom Pomeroy to Borris, in the County Carlow, is
a big jump, but we suppose Peelers’ testimonials make
widely separated places kin. A movement is on foot,
to present a testimonial to Pecler Boyle, late sergeant
in the village of Borris. A committee is already
formed, the majority of whom, as might be suspected,
are Bungs: there are also two house painters exerting
themselves in the noble cause, and the Petty Sessions
Clerk is also interesting himself in this nation-making
movement. So far as we know, the countrymen in
the neighbourhood of Borris have not subscribed, and
we hope they will stick to that attitude and keep their
money for some better purpose. We hear that the
licensing laws during the past five years in Borr
were not kept very well, and that Sunday mornings
and evenings were like Fair days. A few publicans of
the better class are holding aloof from this testimonial,
and the committee are rather reticent about the whole
business. Perhaps those of them who pose as ‘‘strong
Nationalists™ are rather ashamed of the part they are
taking. We hope to be able to get some more infor-
mation about this affair, and the little light we are
able to throw this week on this dark corner of Boiris
activity may do the village good if it does no good to
the testimonial.

MINIMUM WAGES.

W E showed in previous articles that economic

forces at work in the present industrial world
tended to drive those who only have plentiful labour
to sell, and who must sell it or starve, to the limit of
subsistence, and even beyond it, inasmuch as they are
forced to work under wages and conditions that arti-
ficially shorten their lives. The industrialism of to-
day is a vigorous working organism and is highly pro-
ductive: a crying weak point is that it tends to drive
certain unprotected labour to the wall. Trades union-
ism has protected many trades, but there are hordes
of workers unprotected whose wretched lot is in no
adequate way affected by the progress of science,
machinery and wealth. It is a matter of comparative
indifference from the statesmanlike point of view whe-
ther thousands of people have five or four or two hun-
dred pounds a year: those who make money by lucky
and shrewd investments, by ‘business gapacity and
energy, by being owners of choice city sites, rare voices
or special abilities may be left to their fate—and pos-
sibly to new taxation: a great many of them would, no
doubt, be better in soul and body if they had less
money. But it is a question of national and pressing
importance that the unprotected labour that notwith-
standing the wealth of the country is forced to sell
itself too cheaply should be guarded by minimum.
Better let men and women remain idle in a rich coun-
try and be dealt with by some other means than lower
the -currency of labour by letting it hire itself out at
sweabed terms.

Of course you must catch your hare before you cook
it; and there is no use in talking of a minimum wage
if it is not economically to be had. If you said that
every man on the Great Blasket Island should be paid
£1 a week and every woman 10s., an obviously suffi-
cient answer would be that the wealth of the island

. Workers are compelled by uncontrolled competition to

Cheap labour has made for cheap goods, and cheap
goods have increased the real wages of all those who
buy them. So far as increased wages increase the
price of goods the real wages of those who buy these
goods are decreased. Cheapness stimulates. demand
and a rise in price tends to restrict it. 'hese things
have to be kept in mind when we deal with minimum
wages. Modern industrialism has produced a state of

. affairs, and a bad one, out of which it is no simple
matter to emerge. 3

Where are the increased wages to come from? If
.t-hg employers are engaged in a competitive industry
1t is reasonable to suppose that the increased wages
cannot come out of profits, but rather that as they
make for an increase in cost of production they must
80 on to the price and come out of the pockets of the
buying public. In view of the fact of the constant
Improvement of productive machinery of all kinds it
wnight happen that increased wages might not in many
cases involve increased prices but merely retard reduc-
tions in price. Let us take it that an increase of
wages does involve an increase in price, it will hit the
consumer, and by tending to diminish the demand it
may reduce the volume of the industry and so displace
sorae of the labour. Thousands of people are working
at very low wages and a rise in price of certain manu-
factured articles would hit these who had to buy them
and these would find their real wages reduced. A rise
in the wages of any big class of workers means, other
things remaining the same, a decrease in the real wages
of the rest in so far as they are consumers of the pro-
duct of that particular labour. For instance, owing to
readjustments in the coal labour market—the middle-
man may be grabbing too much at the moment, but
if so competition will tend to adjust that—all of us
who use coal have had our real wages reduced owing
to a greater portion of them going in exchange foi' the
coal we are accustomed to consume. If the wages of
those engaged in the various industries concerned in
the turning out of finished linen articles were increased

- and the prices of the articles were forced up in conse-
quence all of us who use linen would have our real
wages reduced.

For each industry there is the problem—-how much,
if any, diminution of consumption, will result from an
increased price owing to increased wages, and how
much labour, if any, will consequently be displaced ?
Then there is the further complication of foreign com-
petition. The State could make a minimum wage
compulsory within its own jurisdiction but it cannot
force it on other countries. If a foreign country with
low standards of wages undersells an industry ham-
pered by comparatively high wages at home, what is
to happen when trade is free and when people will buy

. in the cheapest market? But already home indus-
tries are *‘ hampered "’ by all ‘sorts of regulations that
~add to the cost of running factories and a minimum
wage for certain classes of labour would be only one
more additional regulation. But at the worst if a
country could not keep an industry except at the price
of resorting to sweafing we should say better give up
the industry and turn the displaced labour into some
more favourable channel. No industry is an asset to
a country that involves thousands of its people slowly

THE CENSUS SUMMARIES.
g (Orange Bluft About Ulster.)

(N October 17th, Mr. Moore, M.P., in the House of

- Commons, dealing with the Government pro-
posal to hand over the administration of the Irish Post
Office to the Irish Parliament, delivered himself as
follows :— ; 2

““T do not think that a Dublin Parliament, 75 per
cent. of whose members would represent people on the
land, would be much concerned about letters. I ven-
ture to assert that if you had an Irish Parliament this
proposal would be carried :—That the best way of in-
creasing the revenue would be to charge an cxtra_half-
penny  on  English letters.’””  He represented his
Nationalist opponents as reasoning thus:—

““Why not get it out of the North? After all we 75
per cent. engaged in agriculture do not bother very
much about letter-writing, and we are not very much
concerned about newspapers, but those wretched fel-
lows in Belfast are writing letters every day.’’

Of course, argument of this description was not in-
‘tended for the Irish public, or for the consideration of
ithe Irish members, who took no notice of it, although
present in force.  Its object was to rub in the notion
so widely prevalent outside Ireland, that the inhabi-
tants of Leinster, Munster and Connaught are almost
exclusively agriculturists, and that the only portion of
Treland having manufactures and commerce is the *‘ pro-
-sperous North.”” s

But does the following table,. constructed from the
xecently published Census Summaries, support this
impression : —

Professional. Domestic. Commercial,
Leinster 58,941 66,972 43,661
Munster 35,619 44,975 21,351
Ulster 34,823 47,466 42,248
Connaught 11,751 11,336 3,883
141,134 170,749 111,143

The population of Ireland being 4,390,219 and that of
Ulster 1,581,696, a very simple calculation discloses the
fact that Ulster, to be on a level with the rest of Ire-
land, should have 50,847 persons engaged in the various
professions, but has only 34,823. That she should have
61,511 domestic servants, but has only 47,466. That
she should have 40,042 persons connected with trade
and commerce instead of 42,248. Leinster and Munster
are heavily ~weighted with Connaught; but even so,
Ulster’s comimercial predominance is not striking!

As compared with Leinster, Ulster’s position, in face
of the preposterous claims put forward in her behalf, is
simply grotesque. With a population exceeding that of
Leinster by 420,000, Leinster has a slightly larger num-
ber of persons connected with commerce; more than
half as many again in the professions; and an extra
20,000 domestics. ¥

Leinster’s superiority over Ulster is in these respects
more remarkable when precisely put. Leinster’s popu-
lation being 1,162,044 and Ulster's 1,581,696, it follows
that Leinster having 58,941 persons connected with the
various professions, Ulster, unless inferior in status,

- should have 80,227 engaged in them instead of only

34,8231 ILeinster having 66,972 male and female
domestic servants, Ulster should have 91,158 instead
of 47,466 ! Leinster having 43,661 persons enumerated
as engaged in commerce, Ulster should have 59,429
merchants, clerks, shopmen, ete., instead of 42,248.

- I, therefore, follows that it is Leinster, not Ulster, that

would be specially hard hit were an Irish Parliament to
be so idiotic as to put an extra halfpenny on the postage

% o'f,letrters and newspapers.

* Nor need Munster be in the very slightest ‘degree_"
afraid of a comparison with the Northern blusterer.
Her population being 1,085,495 and Ulster’s 1,581,696,

it follows that if Ulster has a professional class number-

ing 84,823, Munster, unless inferior to Ulster, should
have one of 22,798. She has one of 85,619—a larger
one than Ulster’s, notwithstanding the latter’s extra
half-million inhabitants!

Ulster having a domestic class numbering 47,466,
Munster should have one of 29,444. She has actually
one of 44,925, or fifty per cent. more than her populys
tional proportion! In trade and commerce Ulster, as
regards Munster, comes out slightly better. Her com-’
mercial class numbering 42,248, Munster’s should bhe
217,659, but is only 21,351. ; 2

On the whole, however, Munster has clearly the best
of the comparison: With a much greater proportion of
her population connected with the professions, and fifty
per cent, more of ifi in a position to employ ‘domestic
servants, Munster would be much harder hit than Ulster
by the imposition of extra taxation.

In a comparison with Connaught, however, Ulster
scores heavily! If Connaught’s population (610,984)
were equal socially to that of Ulster, the little province
should have 13,452 professionals instead of 11,751—
18,335 domestics instead of 11,336, and 16,319 commer-
cials instead of 8,883. That this is the sort of differ-
ence, not between Ulster and Connaught, but between
Ulster and the rest of Ireland, is the impression
Unionist orators usually seek to convey even in the
House of Commons itself in presence of the Irish Mem=
bers. Thus, Mr. W. Moore, M.P., in the speech from
which we have already quoted, gave utterance to the
following : —

““ The very instant an Irish Parliament wanted
to raise a little revenue they would say as they have
always said : ‘ Let us get it out of Belfast.’

‘“ Hon. Members—' We have never said any:
thing of the sort.”

*“ Never said it! Why the reason you would nct
agree to leave out the Ulster counties was because
you would have nothing to tax. That is a truism.’’

And Mr. Moore, M.P., is right! Outside of Ireland
there is no more generally received opinion than that
Ulster’s actual position, relative to Connaught, is that
of Ulster to the three other provinces! Mr. Moore,
afer all, was only quoting from the National Revicw,
which had reproduced, as a magazine article, a speech
delivered by the Rt. Hon, F. E. Smith at Belfast, on
the 12th July last, and in which he stated that Home
Rule was not wanted without Ulster, as in that case
there would bé nothing to tax!
Although the Irish Members indignantly interruptad
the Orange orator, not a man of them attempted to
repel his extravagant pronouncements, which are now
enshrined in the Official Report of the House of Com-
mons debates, and will, of course, be accepted as strictly
accurate, in the complete absence of contradiction, bsr
whoever happens to-refer to them in the future.
Barlier in the evening Mr. T. M. Healy made the
following reference to the Ulster figment:— :

““The delusion -is constantly presented in this
House that Ulster is a specially prosperous part cf
the country, and on that account its fat ribs are
likely to be cut into by us. Unfortunately I have
not the figures, but on the occasion when the last
Home Rule Bill of Mr. Gladstone was under discus-
sion I then had the figures, and these are the facts
—that Dublin paid a larger income tax than Bel-
fast, and that Leinster is richer than Ulster.””

DISINFECTING THE PICTURE SHOWS.

"l‘H-E picture shows need careful watching. Their
i influence is wide-reaching, and in many ways it
is & harmful influence. They are anti-national. Their
effect is often baneful to the spectators, more especially
to the young. They deepen the poverty of the puor;
they create and foster habits of thriftlessness among
the young people of our towns and cities ; they bid
fair to ruin the legitimate business of small traders.
Worst of all, they have recently embarked, with cynical
effrontery, on a course of Sunday profanation.

The picture shows are un-Irish and anti-national. I
they * survey mankind from China to Peru,” they manage
%o leave Ireland almost wholly outside their purview.

_ Morbid scenes of sensationalism and romance, episodes
of war and crime, dramas of jealousy and revenge—these
form the staple of their stock-in-trade. It is all a com-
bination of pictorial penny dreadful and cheap melo-
drama. These exhibitions foster in their Irish spectators
an interest in every country under heaven except their
own. What wonder if the boys and girls of our cities
grow up with minds denationalised and depraved ?

Under happier auspices the picture shows might do

rast deal towards creating a national spirit. They

ht do highly meritorious work in the way of imbuing

Irish people with a love for their Motherland, an enthu-

siasm for her scenery, her history, her monuments, and

a living interest in her industries and institutions.

It is a startling fact that there are picture shows on
the further side of the globe, twelve thousand miles away,
which are incomparably more Irish than those of Dublin.
By way of example, and to afford a glimpse of what our
picture palaces might do if they cared to, I quote the
following advertisement from a recent issue of the
Adelaide Soulhern .Cross :—

This announcement tells its own story. It shows that
-the Irish residents of Adelaide, though only a fraction
.of the city’s population, have had sufficient grit and
-enterprise to induce an Australian picture palace to
provide Irish films, Irish' songs, Irish dances and to
secure the services of an Irish pipers’ band. )

Public opinion should compel the managers of cinema
theatres in Ircland to copy this laudable example, and
“to improve upon it. Public opinion should force them
to exhibit plenty of Irish films; it should constrain them
“to cater for Irish sentiment. They should be made to
realise that the Irfsh public will not tolerate everything
which Englishmen presume to dump upon our shores.

When shall we hear of a Dublin cinematograph thezt.e
devoting a night’s takings to the Gloucester Street
Relfuge, or the Denmark Street Orphanage, or to any
one of the dozens of Catholic charities which sorely need
a ance? Not, perhaps, until some public-spirited
Irishman “shall enter the cinema business, and shall
compel his rivals, by force of prestige and good example,
to pursue a less selfish and less anti-national policy.

At present the picture shows often exert a pernicious
influence upon the spectators, particularly upon the
young. Pictures tainted with il suggestiveness are
frequently shown upon the screen. Some of the picture
dramas lately shown in Dublin are said to have fairly
recked with suggestiveness. One of them depicted the
efforts of a scoundrel to ruin a young girl.

A few weeks ago the Dublin Vigilance Committee
felt bound to make a public protest against certcin
indecent films which were being exhibited in the cit
The films, in consequence, were withdrawn. The manager
of one of the Dublin picture shows told the Vigilance
Committee’s representatives on that occasion that
deliberate efforts were being made, from across the
water, to introduce * smutty ” pictures into this country.
This manager declared that he had pointed out that a
certain indecent film would surely * raise a row il
exhibited in Dublin; it was exhilil_tctl in Kingstown
and there was no row. The Catholics of Kingstown, it
appears, were too tame to raise their veice in protest
against imported pictorial filth. It is horrible to think
of the way in which the minds of the young are being
defiled and their morals debased by pictures of this
kind.

There are priests in Dublin who spend themselves
in striving to uplift and ameliorate the moral and social
condition of the children of our slums. Huge sodalities
for boys have been organised by these earnest and
devoted men ; employment burcaus have been opened
and savings banks instituted. All this has been done
for the purpose of promoting religion, decency and thrift
among the hordes of poor hoys who swarm 1 the city’s
slums. G

Now come the picture halls, with their meretricious
attractions, to undo all this good work. These shows
too often defile the minds of the young and deprave
their morals. They fill the minds of children with
sensationalism of the * penny dreadful ** order ; and we
all know how pernicious is the effect of penny dreadfuls
upon the youthful mind. Such shows, besides, are
guilty of promoting deplorable habits of thriftlessness
among the young people of our cities and towns.

" One cannot walk through Dublin’s streets without
being continually pestered for pennies by grubby little
ragamuffins. These miserable street arabs look as il
they had not eaten a square meal for a week; yet in
very many cases the pennies they have begged or stolen
during the day go to swell the receipts of the picture
“*halls in the evenuing.

Surely it is far from creditable to Dublin that such
a multitude of ragged urchins, capless and shoeless and
dirty, should be allowed to spend the livelong day in
the streets, begging in one guise or another. One wonders
what on earth the school attendance inspectors are doing,
and what on earth the police are doing. The police
turn a blind eye to all this street begging, though they
surely must know that it-is both a disgrace to the city
and a violation of the law. The Dublin police seem to
imagine that it is simply their office to be an ornament
to the landscape. They seldom or never, apparently,
interfere with this ignoble practice of street begging,
or with the brawling of drunken women, or with certain
horrors which must be nameless here.

Instead of being suffered to spend their days on the
streets and their evenings in the picture halls, the grimy
gamins of our city should he compelled to attend school,
or else they should be taken up by the police and com-
mitted to some industrial institution. Therein lies
practically their only chance of becoming decent citizens,

s good Catholics, and worthy men. If these wandering
5 street arabs are neglected alike by parents, police and
magistrates, how can we expect them to become a credit
> to their religion and an asset to their country ? How,
H indeed, can we expect them to be anything in after-life
£ but the parasites and pests of society? It were better
a thousand times that these ragged youngsters should be
| taken away from their drunken parents and placed in
! some <Catholic industrial school. There at least they
i would be well clothed and well fed; they would be
: carefully instructed in their religion; they would be
taught some useful trade and fittcd to become reputable
members- of society.

THE BALKAN WAR—ITS ORIGIN AND
3 PURPOSE.

By R. J. KeLry, B.L.

AST June and July I had the honour and privilege
of attending at Prague, the capital of the Czech
kingdom of Bohemia, a most representative and, as
events proved, a most portentous gathering of the
leading men of the great Slav States, notably Bulga--
rians, Servians, Montenegrins and Bohemians. It
was an unique gathering in many respects. It was the
largest meeting of these land-divided but racially-uni-
ted races ever before held, and it was the first occasion
on which Serb and Bulgar had met on a common plat-
form.  Now they are fighting side by side on the
bloodiest battlefields of our time and covering them-
selves with immortal glory. There were other Slavs
at that significant and impressive meeting.  There @
were altogether some 30,000 to 40,000 Poles, Russians,
Croatians, Dalmatians, Macedonians, and countless
others of the Slav race, but a common purpose ani-
mated them—the determined resurgence of the Slav,
his rise from serfdom to sovereignty, his -evolution
from being ruled to being a ruler. He has gone through
his long and tedious process of evolution. ~He has
boine the burden of servitude, worn the badge of sla-
very for centuries, but at long last he has resolved to
ise from the sleep of ages, and he is now with magni-
ficent courage asserting himself. After all, nineteen
millions of people, however locally separated, cannot
be without weight in the councils of Europe. I knew
great things were coming when I saw those men and
watched their movements—saw the air of determina-
tion and vesolve that was in their eye, how they
marched through the streets, how they went through
the exercises of the Sokols. These men, I said to my- -
self, were not made to be slaves, and slaves no longer
they will be! But a few months have since elapsed
and in the interval the entire map of Eastern Europe
has been re-cast. The Slavs have, thank God, trium-
phed. Bulgaria is once again a power and a State of
great strength and resources. It was so in the fifth
century, when its armies, as are its armies to-day,
bivouacked in the neighbourhood of Constantinople,
and made its rulers quake on their thrones. Servia was
once a great power, and will, Heaven be praised, be so
again. It has regained its old capital of Uskub—won
it in fair fight, by the sacrifice of its brave soldiers, and
what it so dearly won it will not surrender. Pitt said
after Austerlitz, ““ Roll up the map of Europe.” So
may our modern statesmen. As we knew its political
geography a few weeks ago Eastern Europe will never
be again. Macedonia will be free. So will Old Servia,
Crete, and the ofher places so long under the heel of
the Turk. He has to go bag and baggage out of the
fair provinces he so long defiled. He may be per-
mitted to remain in Constaninople for the present, but
out of it sooner or laterrhe must go. As a ruler he is
in these days impossible. The conscience of the en-

Great Powers, and his reign of bloodshed, his regime
of slaughter is at an end. His Kurds and Bashi-
Bazouks must retire from burning and murdering and
massacring Buropean peoples and go back to their
Asiastic homes.  This is the outcome of the pre-
sent bloody but glorious war. If ever war is just, this
is just. If ever war is holy, this is holy. To relieve
people from servitude, to bring peace and happiness to
countless households, to save human life and Christian
virtue, that is the aim and object and that blessed
purpose th:ls war will, please God, attain. The trium-
phm;ﬁ armies of the brave allied forces—the dauntless
warriors of Montenegro, Bulgaria, Servia and Greece
are everywhere covering themselves with immortal
glory. Never in history was there such a splendid in-
stance of superb courage, splendid discipline, and un-
wavering military success. Irom the first encounter
they succeeded. Victory after victory, success after suc-
cess followed their path and crowned their actions.
Town, fort, city and outpost, it mattered nothing. All
went down before the all-conquering, all-victorious
allies. Napoleon’s best battles were nothing to General
Ivanoff’s, and the other Bulgarian, Servian, Montene-
grin and Greek leaders who have raised themselves to
the pinnacle of now being the best soldiers in Turope.
After all, it is but as it should be, that the Turk should
go. He never was acclimatized in Europe although
here for five hundred years. He never regarded himself
at home here—he only looked upon himself as a so-
journer settled here for a time. Lord Houghton in
his Eastern Poems very beautifully and truly expresses
that idea of isolation. Thus we read ——

** When first the Prophet’s standard rested on
The land that once was Greece and still was Rome
We deemed that his and our dominion
Was there as sure as in our Eastern home!
We never thought a single hour to pause
Till the wide West had owned Mahommed’s laws.”

And he goes on to say how could they doubt their
omnipotence when they found everything going down
before them and submitting to their rule—

““The dome to heavenly wisdom consecrate

Still echoes with the Muslim’s fervent prayers;

The just successor of the Khaleefate

Still on his brow the sign of empire wears

We hold our wealth without reserve or fear,

And yet we know we are but tented here.

Millions of Christians bend beneath our rule,

And yet these realms are neither theirs nor ours.

.Sultan and subject are alike the tool

Of Burope’s ready guile or banded powers
Against the lords of continent and sea,
What can one nation do, one people be.”

These lines fairly and accurately describe the Turk.

He never felt at home in Kurope. Round and about
him were dispossessed peoples, alien to him in blood,
language and religion. He had nothing in common
with them, nor they with him. He kept them down
by fire and sword, but they have risen up and thrown
him off. Bit by bit he surrendered the vast territory
he won and could not keep, and the sooner he gets
bu]([:l: to Asia the better. He may well say to him-
self i—

““ Leb us return to Asia’s fair domain,

Let us in truth possess the Bast again.

Leb us return! across the fatal strait.

Our fathers’ shadows welcome us once more
Back to the glories of the Khaleefate, 4
Back to the faith we loved, the dress we wore,
When in one age the world could well contain
Haroon Il Rasheed and your Charlemagne.”’

This will be the end of the war. Out of Macedonia the:
Turk, as a ruler, goes, and goes in God’s name. He
may remain here and there as a settler and farmer, for
under the Bulgarian and Servian he will have peace
and quiet, as his brethren have to-day, but while as a
citizen and co-worker he may be let alone and left at
rest, as a ruler he is doomed, and never again, ** while
grass grows,”’ will he hold the fair province of Mace-
donia and Old Servia in his blood-stained grasp. Never
again will its fields be the playground of his savage
Khurds and Bashi-Bazcuks, ‘* to rifle, ravage and
rape.”” This is what the present war has brought
about and what those brave soldiers of the Allies have
effected, at terrible sacrifice no doubt, but with a
courage that never was equailed in the annals of war.
ying that fine, they cannot submit to having the
fair provinces dragged from the grasp of the Turk
handed back to him to murder and massacre iu as is his.
wont. This was done before when the ill-fated Treaty
of Berlin forced back under the Turk Macedonia and
the other Christian districts, and at least 50,000 lives.
have paid the penalty of the swrender.  How pro-
phetic was Mr. Gladstone’s warning in 1876 when with
an energy and an eloquence that roused not only Eng-
land but Europe, he then said what the armed forces
of the Allies are to-day saying with more effect, ** Let
the Turks now carry away their abuses in the only
possible manner—by carrying off themselves, their
Zaptieks and their Mudirs, their Bembashes and {heir
Guzbashes, their Raimakams and their Pashas, one
and all, bag and baggage, shall T hope clear out front
the province they have desecrated and profaned.’”
Surely grand words. Would they were listened to and
the advice taken. But alas, 36 years of woe upon woe
have been added to the long tale of persecution, and
what European diplomacy failed fo effect the artillery
of the four allied Powers has, and the Turk is on the:
run at last. In the immortal words of Peter the Her-
mit when preaching the first Crusade, ‘“‘God wills it.””

Dear Sir,—I have read some statements in your
paper with regard to Miss Brodrick, and should like
to ask a few questions for Miss Brodrick or her secre-
tary to answer.
. 1. Did Miss Brodrick select the site of her hospital
with the approval of Dr. James Trant, in whose dis-
pensary distvict it is situated?  Fas Miss Brodrick
stated how her hospital is to be kept up when finished ?
Will she give a guarantee that it will not, wholly or
partly, be put on the rates?

2. Has she at any time given a guarantee that her
building will be a hospital for the poor? Will she

. now do so? 2

3. Did she, at any time, send reports of alleged
neglect of duty on the part of the Jubilee nurse to her
committee which on investigation were found to be
false ?

4. When, in her reports and letters, she speaks of
“we,”” whom does she mean? Will she give the
names of her hospital committee ?

5. Your correspondent states she has spent ‘‘over
£9,500 on her work in Kerry” (her own money). She
has, as well, collected money for her hospital.

THE LEADER.

Will she publish a detailed report as to how this
£9,000 and the money collected has been expended ?

6. Why does she state to a Press representative in
New York thab she is collecting money for 20 more
beds in a hospital that is not yet completed ?

7. Did Miss Brodrick herself attend Michael Sulli-
van, Goulanes, or send a visitor who was staying with
her? Why was she obliged to wade in such deep
water when the Jubilee nurse who visited the same
patient on the same day crossed the stream only ankle
deep? The doctor also visited the patient that day
and ordered him to hospital.

8. Will she state how many visits she has paid to
patients since January 1st (the Jubilee nurse has paid
1,121 visits since that date) ? Will Miss Brodrick alsc
state how many midwifery cases she has attended
since coming to the district in 1908 ?

I have no wish to belittle Miss Brodrick’s acts of
heroism, but it seems a pity she is not more consis-
tent in them. Two years ago she refused to go less
than two miles to attend Mrs. John Gallivan, Brack-
arragh, in her confinement, the doctor and other trained
help being 12 miles away. By the time the doctor
arrived, Mrs. Gallivan (the mother of six little chil-
dren) was dead.
